content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 7, 2014


Mr. Quinn M. Barrow
Richards Watson Gershon
355 South Grand Avenue, 40th Floor
Los Angeles, CA 90071-3101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-14-084(b)

Dear Mr. Barrow:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Tony D'Errico regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ We offer no opinion on the application of other laws, which may apply, such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

1. Does Councilmember D'Errico have a conflict of interest that prohibits him from voting on an extension of the interim ordinance that was at issue in your first request for advice, Advice Letter 14-084(a)?

2. Does Councilmember D'Errico have a conflict of interest that prohibits him from participating in a decision to sign a contract with the Urban Land Institute?

CONCLUSIONS

1. No. You have not provided facts to suggest that the upcoming decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on Mr. D'Errico's financial interests or to change our analysis from Advice Letter No. 14-084(a).

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-14-084(b)
Page No. 2

2. No. The decision to contract with ULI will not have a reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on Councilmember D'Errico's leasehold interest.

FACTS

You are the City Attorney for the City of Manhattan Beach (the "City") and have been
authorized by Tony D'Errico, a member of the Manhattan Beach City Council, to request
additional advice following your request earlier this year. (Barrow Advice Letter No. 14-
084(a).) The facts are largely unchanged and we incorporate them here by reference. At issue in
that letter was the city council's vote on an interim ordinance to be in place throughout the
development of Manhattan Beach's redevelopment. While the Downtown Plan is developed,
there is concern that the unchecked expansion or addition of non-retail uses could frustrate the
purposes of the plan. To prevent this, the proposed Ordinance prohibits any change in land use
during the period that the Downtown Plan is being developed. There would be two exceptions to
the Ordinance. First, like-for-like changes (such as changing one restaurant for another or one
retailer for another) would be allowed by right with no additional discretionary review by the
City. Second, a change in land use would be permitted upon the approval of a conditional use
permit by the City's planning commission.

Before the city council at this time is an expansion of that ordinance to apply to any chain
(or "formula") stores that seek entry into the downtown area. The ordinance would state that any
such store would require a use permit before the Planning Commission during the study period
even if the type of store were similar to the existing store. For example, if a property owner
owns a retail space and it is occupied by a local clothing retailer, the owner would have to apply
for a use permit during the development period if the owner wanted to lease the space to the
international chain store, the Gap.

An additional decision before the city council will be whether to approve a contract to
engage the Urban Land Institute ("ULI") to perform a focused analysis of downtown Manhattan
Beach. Established in 1936, the Institute has more than 30,000 members worldwide,
representing the entire spectrum of the land use and development disciplines. ULI will spend a
week in the city with a team of advisors who assess the current state of the area, listen to
civic leaders about their goals, and develop a plan going forward. The staff has recommended
ULI based on its proven history, unbiased analyses, and comprehensive methodology.

Councilmember D'Errico and his wife jointly lease two properties located roughly in the
center of the Downtown Plan area. The D'Erricos operate separate retail clothing stores on each
property. The lease agreements for both properties are substantially the same in the following
material respects:

• The premises are leased for the sole use as retail clothing stores, and no other use is
permitted without the landlord's prior written consent.

File No. A-14-084(b)
Page No. 3

• The original terms of the leases are from May 1, 2011, through April 30, 2014. The D'Erricos have exercised an option to extend the leases for three years through April 30, 2017. (The D'Erricos have an additional three-year option for both leases.) In your email dated July 2, 2014, you stated that the Councilmember has operated his clothing business at that location for seven years.

• Rent under both leases is subject to an escalator based on the Consumer Price Index.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).)

Regarding your first question, the analysis from the Barrow Advice Letter, No. A-14-084(a) applies. There, we found that there would be no reasonably foreseeable financial effect on Councilmember Barrow's leasehold interest, the clothing stores, or the retail customers. Under the additional facts you provided, the city council will determine whether to extend the interim ordinance to require use permits if a chain or formula store were to desire to rent current retail space. This additional to the interim ordinance does not change the lack of reasonable foreseeability address in our initial letter to you.²

Regarding your second question on whether Councilmember D'Errico may participate in the decision to contract with ULI, our response is similar. That letter found that the ordinance would maintain the status quo until the city council settled on a downtown plan. The decision here is even more removed as it will not actually do anything except inform future decisions (decisions in which Councilmember D'Errico could have a conflict of interest). In the first letter, we analyzed each economic interest and concluded that the interim ordinance would not create a conflict for the councilmember. For there to be a conflict of interest under the Act, it must be reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a material financial effect on the official's economic interest. The contract at issue will allow ULI to spend a week in Manhattan Beach engaging in an in-depth analysis of the current state of the city, determining the city leaders' and stakeholders' goals, and recommendations of what steps to take to achieve those goals. Once ULI's report is completed, the city council will then determine in what direction it would like to go with a downtown development plan.

The applicable foreseeability rule is: "A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public

² For a complete analysis of the economic interests, see Barrow Advice Letter No., 14-084(a).

File No. A-14-084(b)
Page No. 4

official's control, it is not reasonably foreseeable." (Regulation 18706(b).) We do not see that it
is reasonably foreseeable that a decision to gather information and obtain a neutral opinion (or
not to) would have a material financial effect on the councilmember's economic interests. Under
the facts presented, the decision is not based on any particular outcome, and in fact the literature
that ULI provided states that its analyses and conclusions vary greatly depending on the
jurisdiction. An effect on Councilmember D'Errico's financial interests is not foreseeable absent
extraordinary circumstances.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

[signature]
By: Heather M. Rowan
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within 
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) 
Regarding your first question, the analysis from the Barrow Advice Letter, No. A-14-
084(a) applies. There, we found that there would be no reasonably foreseeable financial effect 
on Councilmember Barrow's leasehold interest, the clothing stores, or the retail customers. 
Under the additional facts you provided, the city council will determine whether to extend the 
interim ordinance to require use permits if a chain or formula store were to desire to rent current 
retail space. This additional to the interim ordinance does not change the lack of reasonable 
foreseeability address in our initial letter to you. 2 
Regarding your second question on whether Councilmember D'Errico may participate in 
the decision to contract with ULI, our response is similar. That letter found that the ordinance 
would maintain the status quo until the city council settled on a downtown plan. The decision 
here is even more removed as it will not actually do anything except inform future decisions 
(decisions in which Council member D'Errico could have a conflict of interest). In the first letter, 
we analyzed each economic interest and concluded that the interim ordinance would not create a 
conflict for the councilmember. For there to be a conflict of interest under the Act, it must be 
reasonably foreseeable that a decision will have a material financial effect on the official's 
economic interest. The contract at issue will allow ULI to spend a week in Manhattan Beach

financial result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public
